summary: The second President of Indonesia (1967-1998), a military dictator who rose to power after blaming the failed 1965 coup by the 30 September Movement on the Communist Party (PKI) and, with CIA backing, launched a mass anti-communist purge that killed an estimated one to one-and-a-half million people. Ruling under the banner of the "New Order," he built a security state around the military's "dwifungsi" dual-function policy and the Golkar Party, liberalized the economy with American-educated technocrats known as the "Berkeley Mafia" while presiding over vast KKN corruption, invaded and brutally occupied East Timor from 1975, and resigned in 1998 amid the Asian financial crisis.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Suharto is identified in giveaways: the second President of Indonesia, who succeeded Sukarno and ruled as a military dictator from 1967 to his resignation in 1998, one of the 20th century's longest-serving authoritarian rulers at 32 years in power. His name is also transliterated "Soeharto."

clues.clue: The second President of Indonesia, ruling from 1967 to 1998 after succeeding Sukarno
examples: name this Indonesian president from 1967 to 1998 who succeeded Sukarno.
name this leader who succeeded Sukarno to become the second president of Indonesia.

clues.clue: A longtime military dictator of Indonesia who ruled for 32 years
examples: name this successor to Sukarno, the longtime military dictator of Indonesia.
name this 32-year dictatorship in Indonesia.

works.name: The 1965 Coup and Anti-Communist Purge
works.description: Suharto's path to power began with the 30 September Movement, a failed 1965 coup in which six army generals were killed and thrown down a well; he blamed the killings on the Communist Party (PKI) and, backed by the CIA, used the ensuing chaos to sideline Sukarno and dismantle his "Guided Democracy." The Supersemar Decree of March 1966 granted Suharto the authority to do whatever he deemed "necessary" to restore order, which he used to launch a year-long anti-communist purge that killed an estimated one to one-and-a-half million suspected communists and suppress the PKI outright, while also crushing separatist agitation in Aceh. He did not formally become president until 1967-68, when his "Functional Group" took power six years after the Supersemar. His official account of the coup was challenged by historians Ruth McVey and Benedict Anderson in the "Cornell Paper," and later scrutinized in John Roosa's book Pretext for Mass Murder; his elite Kopassus special forces, employed in his rise to power, later ended the hijacking of Garuda Flight 206 by the group Komando Jihad.

clues.clue: The 30 September Movement, a failed 1965 coup in which six army generals were killed and thrown down a well
examples: Six of this man's fellow generals were murdered and thrown down a well in a failed coup attempt against him.

clues.clue: Blamed the coup on the Communist Party (PKI) and, backed by the CIA, used it to seize power

examples: This man employed the Kopassus special forces in his rise to power, and that group stopped the hijacking of Garuda Flight 206 by Komando Jihad.

works.name: The New Order: Ideology and Repression
works.description: Suharto called his government the "New Order" (Orde Baru), explicitly contrasting it with Sukarno's "Old Order." Its central institutional feature was "dwifungsi" ("dual function"), a policy that rejected General Abdul Haris Nasution's proposed "middle way" and instead gave the military (ABRI) a built-in role in both security and civilian government, including reserved parliamentary seats. Suharto's political vehicle was the Golkar Party, organized by his subordinate Ali Murtopo and headed by Suharto himself before he took power; it adopted the state philosophy Pancasila, which Suharto pursued an "indigenization" of to eliminate Western influence and declared the nation's "single principle," a move the 1980 Petition of Fifty protested. Suharto's security state included the Kopkamtib force, which arrested the founder of the human rights group Tapol, and his personal Aspri advisory group, disbanded after the 1974 Malari Incident — a riot triggered by a state visit from Japanese Prime Minister Kakuei Tanaka that led Suharto to limit foreign investment and promote local ownership of firms. His government held the "Act of Free Choice" plebiscite to circumvent the New York Agreement and annex West Papua, promoted "pribumi" (indigenous) interests akin to Malaysia's bumiputera policy while targeting the ethnic Chinese minority, and in the 1996 "27 July incident" sent red-shirted attackers to storm the headquarters of the opposition PDI, then led by Megawati Sukarnoputri, the daughter of his predecessor.

clues.clue: His government's official name, contrasted with Sukarno's "Old" version
examples: Suharto used this term to refer to his administration, in contrast to Sukarno's "Old" version of it.
Name this world leader who employed the Berkeley Mafia and called his government the New Order.

clues.clue: The "dwifungsi" ("dual function") policy gave the military (ABRI) a built-in role in both security and civilian government, including reserved parliamentary seats

works.name: Economic Policy and Corruption
works.description: Suharto liberalized Indonesia's economy with the guidance of American-educated technocrats nicknamed the "Berkeley Mafia," who were vetted through presentations at the SESKOAD army college, and pursued development through five-year investment plans called REPELITA. His regime became infamous for what opponents dubbed KKN — "corruption, collusion, and nepotism" — including kickbacks from state monopolies disguised as donations to yayasans (foundations) ostensibly for building rural schools and hospitals, his crony Bob Hasan the "plywood king's" negotiations with the fraudulent Canadian gold mining firm Bre-X, and a purchase of dilapidated East German warships whose exposure led the regime to ban magazines like Editor and Tempo. His regime's "business pillar," the Salim Group conglomerate, became a target of the anti-Chinese riots that accompanied his fall.

examples: This government developed free-market economic policies under the guidance of the "Berkeley Mafia."

works.name: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor
works.description: Suharto invaded East Timor in 1975, beginning a bloody occupation that lasted until 1999, opposed by the guerrilla group FRETILIN and its military wing FALINTIL and supported diplomatically by Henry Kissinger. A minor incursion preceding the full invasion, Operation Seroja, killed a group of Australian journalists known as the Balibo Five, whose deaths the Australian government was accused of covering up. The most notorious episode was the 1991 Santa Cruz massacre, in which Indonesian soldiers killed roughly 250 pro-independence protesters in a Dili cemetery, filmed by journalists including Max Stahl and Christopher Wenner; Michele Turner later collected oral testimonies from the occupation's victims in a book titled Telling. Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo and Jose Ramos-Horta won the 1996 Nobel Peace Prize for their efforts to resolve the conflict, and East Timor finally achieved independence in 2002.

works.name: Downfall: the 1997-98 Asian Financial Crisis
works.description: Suharto's rule collapsed alongside the 1997 Asian financial crisis, which began when Thailand's currency, the baht, collapsed and spread to send Indonesia's rupiah plummeting. Facing riots and a deadly shooting of student protesters at Trisakti University, Suharto resigned the presidency in 1998, ending 32 years in power, and was succeeded by B.J. Habibie, who launched the "Reformasi" period of political reform.

clues.clue: The 1997 Asian financial crisis, which began when Thailand's baht collapsed, sent Indonesia's rupiah plummeting and helped trigger his downfall
examples: Suharto resigned as president after the rupiah plummeted in value as a result of this event, which began in Thailand when the baht collapsed.
Policies of a group of American-educated economists known as the Berkeley Mafia contributed to this leader's resignation during the 1997 Asian financial crisis.

examples: This regime was opposed by Megawati's PDI and followed by B.J. Habibie's Reformasi after falling during the Asian financial crisis.
This leader was succeeded by B.J. Habibie after riots in the wake of the 1997 Asian financial crisis.

comprehensive_summary: Suharto was the second President of Indonesia, ruling as a military dictator from 1967 (formally 1968) until his 1998 resignation — 32 years in power. His rise began with the 30 September Movement, a failed 1965 coup in which six army generals were killed and thrown down a well; Suharto blamed the killings on the Communist Party (PKI) and, with CIA backing, used the ensuing chaos to sideline Sukarno and dismantle his "Guided Democracy." The Supersemar Decree of March 1966 granted Suharto authority to do whatever he deemed "necessary" to restore order, which he used to launch a year-long anti-communist purge killing an estimated one to one-and-a-half million suspected communists, suppress the PKI, and crush separatist agitation in Aceh; his official account of the coup was later challenged by Ruth McVey and Benedict Anderson's "Cornell Paper" and John Roosa's book Pretext for Mass Murder, while his Kopassus special forces, used in his rise to power, later ended the hijacking of Garuda Flight 206 by Komando Jihad. Suharto called his government the "New Order," built around the "dwifungsi" ("dual function") policy that rejected General Abdul Haris Nasution's "middle way" and gave the military ABRI a built-in role in civilian government and reserved parliamentary seats. His political vehicle was the Golkar Party, founded by subordinate Ali Murtopo and headed by Suharto, which adopted the state philosophy Pancasila; Suharto pursued an "indigenization" of Pancasila and declared it the nation's "single principle," prompting the 1980 Petition of Fifty. His security state included the Kopkamtib force, which arrested the founder of the human rights group Tapol, and the Aspri advisory group, disbanded after the 1974 Malari Incident (a riot triggered by a state visit from Japanese PM Kakuei Tanaka that led Suharto to limit foreign investment). His government also held the "Act of Free Choice" plebiscite to annex West Papua, promoted "pribumi" interests akin to Malaysia's bumiputera policy while targeting ethnic Chinese Indonesians, and in the 1996 "27 July incident" attacked the headquarters of the opposition PDI, then led by Megawati Sukarnoputri, daughter of his predecessor. Economically, Suharto liberalized Indonesia with American-educated technocrats known as the "Berkeley Mafia," vetted at the SESKOAD army college, and pursued five-year REPELITA development plans, but his regime became synonymous with "KKN" (corruption, collusion, and nepotism): kickbacks from state monopolies disguised as yayasan donations, crony Bob Hasan's dealings with the fraudulent Bre-X gold mining firm, a scandal over dilapidated East German warships that led to bans on magazines like Editor and Tempo, and the Salim Group conglomerate that became a target of anti-Chinese riots during his fall. Suharto invaded East Timor in 1975, beginning a bloody occupation lasting until 1999 that was resisted by FRETILIN and its military wing FALINTIL and diplomatically supported by Henry Kissinger; a minor incursion, Operation Seroja, killed the Balibo Five Australian journalists, and the 1991 Santa Cruz massacre saw Indonesian soldiers kill roughly 250 protesters in Dili, filmed by Max Stahl and Christopher Wenner (later documented in Michele Turner's oral history Telling); Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo and Jose Ramos-Horta won the 1996 Nobel Peace Prize for seeking to resolve the conflict, and East Timor achieved independence in 2002. Suharto's downfall came with the 1997 Asian financial crisis, which began with Thailand's baht collapsing and sent Indonesia's rupiah plummeting; after a deadly shooting of students at Trisakti University, he resigned in 1998 and was succeeded by B.J. Habibie, who launched the "Reformasi" reform period.
recursive_suggestions: Sukarno — Suharto's predecessor, first President of Indonesia; clearly warrants (or already has) an independent History topic
The Indonesian occupation of East Timor — has its own answerline with strong independent tossup and bonus-part presence (T7 plus 3 bonus parts); a strong candidate for its own topic
Santa Cruz massacre — recurs across multiple Suharto-answered and East-Timor-answered questions; watch for independent bonus-part presence developing into its own topic
Golkar Party — Suharto's political vehicle, recurring across several questions; candidate for its own Institution-focused topic if independent presence grows
Jose Ramos-Horta — 1996 Nobel Peace Prize co-winner and later President of East Timor; appeared as an independent bonus-part answer (B7P3)
B.J. Habibie — Suharto's successor who launched Reformasi; recurring in multiple questions as the named successor
Megawati Sukarnoputri — opposition leader (PDI) and later President of Indonesia; recurring as Sukarno's daughter and Suharto's opponent
Prabowo Subianto — Suharto's son-in-law (married his daughter in 1983) and Golkar member; appeared with independent detail (banned from the U.S. for East Timor human rights violations) in B2

30 September Movement / the 1965-66 Indonesian mass killings — has independent framing across multiple bonus parts (e.g. B4's "movements named for dates"); could develop into its own topic distinct from Suharto's page
